What Is Peptide Therapy? A Beginner's Guide
Peptide therapy uses short chains of amino acids to signal specific biological processes. Learn how it works, what conditions it's used for, and what to know before starting.

> **SAMPLE POST.** Format reference for the blog writer agent. Delete before deploying. ## What are peptides? Peptides are short chains of amino acids, the building blocks of proteins. Unlike full proteins, peptides are small enough to be absorbed and used by the body to trigger specific biological responses. ## How peptide therapy works A practitioner prescribes a specific peptide based on your health goals. The peptide signals your cells to perform a particular function, such as increasing growth hormone, improving insulin sensitivity, or reducing inflammation. ## Common types - **GLP-1 agonists**: weight management and metabolic health - **Sermorelin / CJC-1295**: growth hormone stimulation - **BPC-157**: tissue repair and recovery - **Thymosin alpha-1**: immune support ## Is it FDA-approved? Some peptides are FDA-approved for specific conditions. Many others are used off-label or compounded. Regulatory status varies by compound and changes frequently. Check our Laws & legal section for current status. ## What to ask a clinic Before starting peptide therapy, ask: 1. Is the prescribing physician licensed in my state? 2. What is the source of the peptides? 3. What monitoring is included? 4. What are the known side effects? ## Bottom line Peptide therapy is a promising but evolving field. Work with a licensed physician, understand the regulatory status, and have realistic expectations.

SAMPLE POST. Format reference for the blog writer agent. Delete before deploying.

What are peptides?

Peptides are short chains of amino acids, the building blocks of proteins. Unlike full proteins, peptides are small enough to be absorbed and used by the body to trigger specific biological responses.

How peptide therapy works

A practitioner prescribes a specific peptide based on your health goals. The peptide signals your cells to perform a particular function, such as increasing growth hormone, improving insulin sensitivity, or reducing inflammation.

Common types

  • GLP-1 agonists: weight management and metabolic health
  • Sermorelin / CJC-1295: growth hormone stimulation
  • BPC-157: tissue repair and recovery
  • Thymosin alpha-1: immune support

Is it FDA-approved?

Some peptides are FDA-approved for specific conditions. Many others are used off-label or compounded. Regulatory status varies by compound and changes frequently. Check our Laws & legal section for current status.

What to ask a clinic

Before starting peptide therapy, ask:

  1. Is the prescribing physician licensed in my state?
  2. What is the source of the peptides?
  3. What monitoring is included?
  4. What are the known side effects?

Bottom line

Peptide therapy is a promising but evolving field. Work with a licensed physician, understand the regulatory status, and have realistic expectations.

> **SAMPLE POST.** Format reference only. Delete before first deploy.

Lede: the legal or regulatory change, who issued it, and when it takes effect. Use one paragraph attributed to the primary source (the notice, rule, statute, or filing itself, not secondhand coverage).

What the rule actually says: 2–3 paragraphs in plain English. Quote or closely paraphrase the operative language; link the primary document. State legal status accurately. Compounded is never FDA-approved (CLAUDE.md rule 4). No editorializing, no predictions beyond what the source states.

Who it affects: clinics, compounding pharmacies, prescribers, and patients, only as far as the source or official guidance spells out.

Key dates and what to watch: comment deadlines, effective dates, scheduled hearings, per the source.
